title: Fed puts Marc Andreessen on a new task force studying AI's effect on jobs and productivity
subtitle: Under Chair Kevin Warsh's monetary-policy review, the Federal Reserve named the a16z co-founder to co-lead one of five external panels, alongside a Stanford economist on leave at Anthropic and Microsoft's Asha Sharma.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: The Federal Reserve announced on July 9 that Marc Andreessen — co-founder of venture firm Andreessen Horowitz — will co-lead a new Productivity and Jobs task force studying how artificial intelligence and other general-purpose technologies are reshaping the US economy. His co-leads are Charles I. Jones, a Stanford economist currently on leave at Anthropic, and Asha Sharma, Microsoft's executive vice president and Xbox CEO. The panel is one of five external task forces created under Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's broader monetary-policy review and is expected to deliver recommendations by the end of 2026.

The mandate, in the Fed's own language, is to 'assess the economic impact of new general-purpose technologies, including artificial intelligence, to inform the Federal Reserve's policy judgments.' In plain terms, the task force is being asked whether AI's effects on productivity, employment, and inflation are large enough — and fast enough — that the Fed should change how it reads incoming data or sets rates. Warsh framed the broader review as necessary because 'the U.S. economy has changed significantly over the last generation, and never more so than right now.'

The appointment is unusual on two axes. First, seating an active venture investor with sizable AI portfolio exposure — a16z has backed OpenAI, Character.AI, Anysphere, Mistral, and many others — on a Fed body that will inform monetary policy raises real conflict-of-interest questions that critics have already flagged. Second, choosing a co-lead who is on leave at Anthropic (Jones) means two of the three panel heads have direct ties to specific frontier AI labs. Supporters argue the Fed needs people who understand where the technology is actually going; skeptics note the Fed has historically kept industry advocates at arm's length from monetary-policy work.

Takeaway for learners: pay attention to who ends up on the panels that write economic policy, not just who ends up in cabinet seats. The Fed's productivity task force will shape how policymakers interpret the next few years of jobs and inflation data — a period when AI's actual macroeconomic footprint is genuinely unclear. If the panel decides AI is materially raising productivity, that gives the Fed room to run the economy hotter without raising rates. If it decides AI is displacing workers faster than jobs are being created, the debate shifts to labor policy. Which lens wins depends heavily on who is holding it.
